For decades we lived and died by rankings and traffic. That worked when a user saw a list and picked a link. But AI Search doesn't return a list. The query fans out, the model synthesizes one answer, and ranking #1 stops mattering if you're not the citation the model trusts. Classic search was a performance channel. AI Search behaves more like a brand channel, so you have to think about performance differently. The mistake I see executives make: they watch classic rankings dip, assume the team is failing, and miss that brand influence is actually climbing. Wrong scoreboard. The teams winning the next 24 months are the ones rebuilding the scoreboard before the old one goes fully dark. The ones who don't will keep optimizing for a channel that's quietly collapsing and call the silence stability. Brendan Hufford and I are getting into all of it live from Cannes Lions for the @Semrush CMO webinar series. linkedin_posts.author: Lily Ray
linkedin_posts.text: Feed post number 1 Lily Ray • 3rd+ Founder of Algorythmic | VP, SEO & AI Search at Amsive 4d • Edited • Follow For the last couple of years, the easiest GEO hack in the book was to publish a "best [category]" listicle and rank yourself #1. It worked to influence AI answers, and in many cases, it still does. But it also might be handing the win to your competitors now, at least on AI Overviews. I pulled 100 B2B "best [category]" queries from Google's AI Overviews across three dates between April and June, and separated two of the main AI search success metrics: getting cited as a source vs. actually getting recommended in the answer. Turns out those are very different outcomes. When a brand's own self-promotional listicle got cited, that brand was left out of the actual recommendation 69% of the time. And the recommendations went to the established competitors they'd listed in their own article. So you write a listicle, name your competitors, call yourself the best, and Google cites you as the source (citation) while recommending everyone else on your list. So basically, you just inadvertently made the case for your competition. Google seems to have decoupled what it cites from who it recommends, and the recommendations appears to be anchored to actual authority (who the rest of the web talks about and links to) rather than how many times you've called yourself #1. If you're a massive, well-known brand you can still get away with it. If you're not, you might be voting against yourself. This isn't happening in a vacuum either. Google demoted a lot of these sites in organic search back in January, the drops got worse during the May core update, and AI Overviews have started adding disclaimers on AI Overviews warning that a category is "saturated with self-proclaimed experts." (Claude does the same thing, for what it's worth.) If AI citations are your main KPI, I'd reconsider. I’d argue that being recommended is much more valuable. Full breakdown on Substack, link in comments. …more 614 105 comments 38 reposts Like Comment Repost Send

linkedin_posts.text: Feed post number 3 Lily Ray • 3rd+ Founder of Algorythmic | VP, SEO & AI Search at Amsive 1w • Edited • Follow I think the next frontier of spam fighting from the search/AI companies is going to hit one of the most popular page types in SEO/GEO right now: comparison and alternative pages. These are pages on your own site that pit your brand against competitors, across the whole brand or a specific product or service (Brand X vs. Y, Product X vs. Y, [Competitor] Alternatives). They've been making the rounds as an SEO/GEO goldmine lately, and "build a lot of these" is advice I keep hearing at conferences and reading in industry blogs and social posts. This is similar to what I've been sharing all year with "listicles" - and I've got a new Substack piece coming out (ASAP!) on how Google and OpenAI are tuning their AI answers to lean less on brands promoting themselves. Stay tuned for that. Comparison pages are trickier and more nuanced. There are genuine use cases for doing them well, especially at a small scale, and I've occasionally recommended them to clients in certain situations. But like everything in SEO: once a tactic works, it becomes popular and people scale it. (File under: "this is why we can't have nice things" 🫠) The "build these pages" advice inevitably leads folks to use AI to spin up as many as possible. I've already advised several companies that launched dozens, even hundreds of comparison/alternative pages (AKA an "SEO pattern") and ran into trouble. For example, a couple sites got hit by Google's late January update this year. The fundamental problem: like any other "review" page, Google already has strict criteria for product reviews. The core requirement is that you prove you actually tried and tested the product, with evidence. For a brand comparing itself to competitors, meeting that bar would mean you've hired your competitor(s) and your own company, and are authentically reviewing your honest experience using their services. How many brands can say they've actually done that? My guess is probably close to 0. What usually ends up happening instead is brands do "research" about their competition, leaning on negative reviews of their competitors or worse - making up incorrect or untrue information to make your own offering look better than theirs. I believe we're already seeing - and will keep seeing - Google, OpenAI and other AI companies look for "objective" 3rd party reviews* instead of leaning on biased brand content when they generate answers. This is ultimately why I think search/AI companies lean so heavily into Reddit discussions, YouTube reviews, Trustpilot, G2, and other major review sites. (*Yes, I know these 3rd party sites are often influenced / manipulated / pay-to-play as well... so that's a big challenge for them too.) So, as always: test, experiment, learn. But my spidey senses say that building these pages at scale - and doing it inauthentically - like trashing your competitor while calling yourself the best - is risky. When too many sites adopt these approaches, they become a liability long-term. …more 517 128 comments 27 reposts Like Comment Repost Send

linkedin_posts.text: Feed post number 5 Lily Ray • 3rd+ Founder of Algorythmic | VP, SEO & AI Search at Amsive 1w • Edited • Follow Here's a little hack I've been using to quickly convert SEO keywords into relevant AI prompts at scale directly in Google sheets. Use the =AI or =Gemini function - plus this prompt I'll share below - to convert your SEO keywords into associated ways that users might type (or ask out loud) that same question into an AI assistant. It's not perfect (is any prompt selection, though?), but what I like about it is that it allows you to take keywords with real search volume (import from Ahrefs, Semrush, Similarweb, etc.) and quickly convert them into AI prompts at scale. Obviously - you can use an LLM to modify the prompt as needed to get a better result. Prompt below: =AI("You are converting a terse SEO search keyword into the natural-language query a real person would actually type or speak to an AI assistant like ChatGPT, Gemini, or a voice assistant. Rewrite the keyword as a single conversational question or request that preserves the exact search intent and intent type (informational, commercial, transactional, or local). Phrase it the way someone would genuinely ask out loud in a full sentence — not in keyword shorthand. Keep any brand, product, or location named in the keyword, but do NOT invent specifics, constraints, or details that aren't already implied. Do not answer the query. Return only the rewritten prompt as plain text — no quotation marks, no preamble, no explanation, no trailing punctuation beyond a question mark. linkedin_posts.author: Lily Ray
linkedin_posts.text: Feed post number 11 Lily Ray • 3rd+ Founder of Algorythmic | VP, SEO & AI Search at Amsive 2w • Follow The worst thing you can do for your AI search visibility is destroy your SEO. I've shared dozens of examples throughout the year of sites quickly losing SEO visibility on Google, with ChatGPT citations following the same trajectory shortly after. I've added a screenshot from my upcoming talks with a couple of examples of this, as well as a similar slide by Natzir Turrado, who recently shared the same thing. (If you don't read Spanish, it says "if you fall in Google, you fall in AI.") Yet many of the things I've seen folks recommending for GEO are bad ideas for SEO. I believe a lot of this stems from new GEO practitioners not having enough SEO experience to make good long-term decisions. SEO isn't something you can learn overnight. You need years of experience to understand the cyclical nature of how once-effective tactics can actually become liabilities once search engines start identifying and treating them as spam. This is especially true for the "too good to be true" tactics that get popularized and shared throughout the industry. I like to assume that the folks on the search engines' spam teams are always watching what we're up to. If you're comfortable with rapid growth for a few months and then crashing on the other side, go for it. But most brands can't take that kind of risk. Years of SEO experience give you the context to anticipate when a too-good-to-be-true tactic is really just a loophole, and to play "defensive" SEO. SEO is as much about what NOT to do as it is about what to do. We've been in a bit of a gold rush with AI search for the past two years, where lots of new approaches have truly worked to manipulate AI responses and citations for a short period. But those tactics are clearly catching up to many sites now. I've been documenting dozens of examples of sites algorithmically crashing or receiving manual actions this year for excessive SEO/GEO-driven spam. Plus, we now have Google employees publicly discouraging "inauthentic mentions" and other GEO hacks, so we know it's on their radar. I'm also working on new research showing that LLMs like ChatGPT and Claude are clearly taking steps to mitigate spam and manipulation in their responses and citations. Stay tuned for that! In my opinion, the best approach is the one that leaves your brand being cited and recommended *after* the AI companies clean up the spam. …more Activate to view larger image, 716 94 comments 72 reposts Like Comment Repost Send

Andre Alpar Put It to Test: The result? It’s not good 👇 * US AI assistants (ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, Copilot, Perplexity, Meta AI) read raw HTML only * Chinese assistants (DeepSeek, ERNIE, Qwen, Kimi) and Mistral render JavaScript. Most US systems requested only the HTML. They never even pulled the script file. But two cases sit in the grey zone between “reads text” and “runs code”. The most uncomfortable lesson has nothing to do with rendering. It is about trust. * Self-reports are unreliable. Perplexity told in chat that it “couldn’t access” the page — while its own crawler had already fetched that exact page and received an HTTP 200. * The pipeline and the answer can disagree. Grok proved a system can run your JavaScript and still answer from the raw HTML. * When you want to know what an AI actually did with your page, trust the server log, never the chatbot’s description of itself. If your important content is injected by JavaScript — client-side frameworks, lazy-loaded sections, content behind tabs that hydrate on load — then for ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, Perplexity and Copilot, that content effectively does not exist. linkedin_posts.text: Feed post number 7 📱 Cindy Krum reposted this Bastian Grimm • 3rd+ Digital Entrepreneur & Keynote Speaker Visit my website 1w • Follow The markdown copy of your site that everyone rushed to ship this year solves the easy half of the problem. Cloudflare's /crawl, llms.txt, edge-rendered markdown: they hand an agent a clean page-by-page read and drop how those pages relate to each other (you could also use a PDF 🤪). Google's data team published the Open Knowledge Format on June 12. It stores knowledge as a directory of linked markdown files, a thin layer of YAML on top (only a type field is required), and plain markdown links between concepts. Those links turn the folder into a graph. The graph is the part a flat copy cannot carry. A mirror tells an agent what each page says. A graph tells it how the concepts sit relative to each other: which framework sits under which tactic, which metric belongs to which dataset. That relationship layer is most of what understanding a brand means. This is the substrate I have been pointing at from stage all year: 𝗠𝗮𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘀 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘀𝘂𝗺𝗲 𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲, 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘃𝗶𝘀𝘂𝗮𝗹 𝗹𝗮𝘆𝗲𝗿 𝗶𝘀 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗽𝗲𝗼𝗽𝗹𝗲, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗮 𝗮𝗿𝗰𝗵𝗶𝘁𝗲𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗱𝗲𝗰𝗶𝗱𝗲𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝘀𝗵𝗼𝘄 𝘂𝗽. OKF is one more machine-readable surface landing on the same ground: markdown plus a little queryable metadata. OKF was built for internal company knowledge, and no agent reads a website-hosted bundle today. It signals where the machine-readable layer is heading and earns nothing this quarter. Still worth building. Writing one forces you to spell out what your brand knows and how the parts connect, and the gaps show up fast. linkedin_posts.text: Feed post number 10 📱 Cindy Krum reposted this Bastian Grimm • 3rd+ Digital Entrepreneur & Keynote Speaker Visit my website 6d • Follow Google's Chrome team just published security guidance that concedes that 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗺𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗹 𝗰𝗮𝗻'𝘁 𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗽 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗺𝗽𝘁 𝗶𝗻𝗷𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗼𝗻 𝗶𝘁𝘀 𝗼𝘄𝗻. Two guides for the WebMCP origin trial. Both name the same two attack vectors agents have to survive: 1️⃣ 𝗠𝗮𝗹𝗶𝗰𝗶𝗼𝘂𝘀 𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗶𝗳𝗲𝘀𝘁𝘀: Hidden instructions planted in tool names, parameters, or descriptions, written to hijack the agent before it touches a single line of your content. 2️⃣ 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗮𝗺𝗶𝗻𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝗼𝘂𝘁𝗽𝘂𝘁𝘀: A trusted tool returning third-party data (reviews, comments, forum posts) with an injection riding inside it. Chrome states plainly that LLMs read instructions and data as one token sequence, so safety can't be guaranteed inside the model. Also, no promise that a smarter model fixes this. Hence defense-in-depth. • Deterministic first: token limits on responses, restricted cross-origin calls, human confirmation, and treating every tool as state-changing unless flagged read-only. • Probabilistic on top: spotlighting untrusted content, injection classifiers, critic models that check a planned call against the user's real intent. WebMCP runs these tools inside your authenticated session. Tool developers mark what's untrusted, agent developers refuse to run it. Neither side can sit it out. To pressure-test any of it, Chrome's own doc points you at Anthropic's Bloom and Petri for adversarial, multi-turn red-teaming. The publications, analysts, and competitors that mention you give it something it can trust without asking you. That's why I treat on-page work as just one lever for AI citations. With optimization roughly equal, the brand that wins is the one AI already trusts through other sources. linkedin_posts.text: Feed post number 10 Kevin Indig • 3rd+ Growth Advisor View my blog 1w • Follow Good prompt tracking starts with sample design. For your average B2B SaaS CRM category, I’d start with ~40 seed prompts: 12 brand, 12 category, and 16 problem prompts. The problem prompts matter because that is where purchase intent usually lives. Then I’d run each prompt 5x per platform every week. ChatGPT, Perplexity, Gemini, and Google AI Overviews should each get their own score because aggregation hides how differently the engines behave. The panel also needs personas. CFO, IT, and marketing buyers evaluate the same CRM category through different criteria, so averaging them together hides where a brand actually wins or loses. The metric set should include mention rate, citation rate, average position, sentiment, and the attributes attached to each mention, all with confidence intervals. That is how prompt tracking becomes more accurate: each answer becomes one sample in a designed panel, not one truth in a dashboard. To figure out what to do instead, here is a process I use: 1) Identify your winning and losing pages 2) Find competitors with the largest keyword overlap 3) Analyze both the URLs gaining and losing visibility across those sites 4) Classify pages by type: product, blog, comparison, research, news, how-to, definition, template, tool, etc 5) Finally, score the content against patterns we see working in Google SERPs - commodity vs. non-commodity, brand, experience, first-party info, freshness, task completion, format, etc Honestly, it's a lot of work, but showing this to clients is typically eye-opening I've done this process manually many times. So when our friends at Ahrefs asked if I wanted to partner to build something with Agent A - I knew this is what I wanted to build The app uses live Ahrefs data to gather all metrics, find competitors, and evaluate content against our predefined criteria. Teaching the AI Engine Pipeline validated by Microsoft Bing and delivered via a series of keynotes for Google Marketing Live 2026. Visit my website 1w • Follow AI has already formed an opinion about your business. The question is whether that opinion is accurate or thin. Every AI engine that might recommend you is looking at your digital footprint and asking three things: what I call the UCD trio. Understandability. Do I know who you are and what you do? Credibility. Do I believe you're good at it? Deliverability. Do I have enough to hand you to the right person at the right moment? All three are fed by five streams: your products and services, your authority content, your brand narrative, your business operations, and everything you do offline that never makes it online. Most businesses are only feeding two or three. The richest material: client reviews, call transcripts, case studies with real numbers, is sitting behind closed doors, invisible to every AI engine trying to evaluate you. And here's the thing I call the Mirror Principle: AI forms its opinion of you from the world's opinion of you. A review your client publishes on their own account carries more weight than the same review you post on your website. Same words. Different weight. The difference is entirely determined by who publishes it. Get all five streams organized and distributed, and your digital footprint compounds with every client you serve. You stop briefing AI about your brand. You start training it. And the brand that gets there first wins. linkedin_posts.text: Feed post number 4 Koray Tugberk GUBUR • 3rd+ CEO and Founder of Holistic SEO & Digital - Fortune 500 SEO and Business Consultant - Topical Authority - Venture Capitalist - Investor View my services 3d • Follow Can LLM-manipulating listicles and brand mentions help local businesses rank better? Yes. And I intentionally use the word “manipulating,” not “optimizing.” Because in this context, we are not only improving a page. We are artificially changing document statistics across the web to create a new consensus around a brand, service provider, and entity. One of the law agency teams I coach is actively applying this. They also recently hired new teammates from our community, which makes the project even more meaningful for me. The method is based on what we call “external topical maps.” In simple terms: Multiple domains create a connected document network. That network repeats, aligns, and reinforces the same attributes, values, claims, services, and entity relationships for a specific local business. From an external publication, you can technically get 3 things: A mention A claim A link In this case, the main focus is not only links. It is mentions and claims. Because mentions and claims help form consensus. And consensus helps search engines and LLMs understand who the brand is, what it does, where it operates, and why it should be trusted. This law firm reached record leads in May, mainly due to stronger local rankings. It is also the same local law firm website I previously shared in the context of visual semantics, which reached rankings with an estimated query value of more than $500,000 in the accident and personal injury space. But there is another important principle here. The project is also building a strong informational section around legal terms, law-related entities, and legal processes. For location-agnostic queries, do not only look at national rankings. For example, if you rank for “types of damages,” the real question is not only: “Do we rank nationally?” The better question is: “Are we getting impressions and clicks from the main city we actually serve?” Because for a local law firm, an informational page is not successful only when it ranks broadly. It is successful when it strengthens local demand, local impressions, and local entity relevance. That is how informational SEO supports local SEO. That is how external topical maps support brand consensus. And that is how mentions, claims, and document networks can influence both search engines and LLMs. I am thinking about explaining some of these principles later without disclosing the website. Treating it as a proxy for topical standing is the most common mistake in SEO, and it sends people chasing links when the real gap is coverage. Topical authority is structural. It is the fit between what a site is about and what a topic demands. Koray Tugberk GUBUR put a formula on it: Historical Data multiplied by Topical Coverage. Both factors, or there is nothing. A site can cover every query in a topic and still hold no authority if no performance history stands behind that coverage. A domain sitting in the low teens for Domain Rating can still own its topic. Coverage and history decide that, not the number on the dashboard. Coverage without internal linking reads as a pile of pages. Coverage with it reads as a topic. Link budgets scale with money. Topical coverage scales with judgment. You can buy your way up a backlink graph. You cannot buy your way into covering a query network with judgment behind every page. linkedin_posts.text: Feed post number 8 Koray Tugberk GUBUR • 3rd+ CEO and Founder of Holistic SEO & Digital - Fortune 500 SEO and Business Consultant - Topical Authority - Venture Capitalist - Investor View my services 1w • Follow What is an Authority Signature? Google has a concept called “Authority Signature,” which appears in a patent that carries “Topic Authority” in its name. The patent “Determining Topic Authority” explains that an author’s expertise, and the author’s signature on a document associated with a specific topic, can increase the topical authority of that document. In other words, Google can evaluate not only the document and the website, but also the person-like entity behind the content. This becomes even more interesting today because Google is reintroducing famous “authors,” “people,” and “answers” in a social-like format through Google Profiles. Google can recognize person-like entities, gather information about them under a specific profile, and define them with expertise signals. According to the patent, the simplified formula for Authority Signature is: Authority Signature Update = Authorship Percentage of Author A for Topic T in Document D × Topical Weight of Topic T in Document D For example, imagine a document covering two topics: Topic A: 70% of the document Topic B: 30% of the document Three authors contributed: For Topic A: User X wrote 80% User Y wrote 20% For Topic B: User Z wrote 100% The authority contribution is calculated like this: User X on Topic A: 0.80 × 0.70 = 0.56 User Y on Topic A: 0.20 × 0.70 = 0.14 User Z on Topic B: 1.00 × 0.30 = 0.30 If User X also writes 100% of Topic A in another document where Topic A has only 0.05 topical weight, User X gains an additional 0.05 authority for Topic A. So, User X’s total authority for Topic A becomes: 0.56 + 0.05 = 0.61 This means the system does not only evaluate whether an author exists. It can also evaluate: Which topic the author contributes to How much of the document belongs to that topic How much of that topical section the author actually authored How consistently the author contributes to the same topic across different documents This is important because the patent mainly defines topic authority for authors, not websites. That shows an early design where authority is treated as an attribute of a person-like entity. Later, this concept can be understood together with site name, site boundary, and website segment concepts. For example, a news website has many different authors. Each author can represent a different topical section of the website. A politics author, health author, finance author, or sports author can contribute different expertise signals to different website segments. Thus, every author’s expertise can strengthen the topical authority of a specific website segment in a specific direction. Topical Authority is not only about how much a website covers a topic. It is also about who covers the topic, how consistently they cover it, and whether the system can recognize the author as a real entity with a measurable topical signature. The company’s main obstacle is competing against highly authoritative provider brands, while large aggregators leverage forum and UGC content with limited visual semantics. In this type of market, the website still has to rank for commercial terms. Thus, we heavily focused on the informational segment of the topical map and used it to support the commercial sections through contextual internal linking. During the project, no backlink-related progress was implemented. According to Microsoft Bing data, the website received over 500,000 impressions from AI search features, along with 28,000 citations. Let’s use “safari” as a replacement example for the actual industry. If the central entity is “safari,” you need to cover all possible attributes and relational contexts around it, such as: Safari + vaccines Safari + luggage Safari + lodge types Safari + routes Safari + seasons Safari + safety Safari + itineraries Safari + local regions Safari + travel requirements All possible predicates and relations around the safari context should be organized as a connected content network. Commercial internal links should point to the review pages, main provider pages, itineraries, routes, and engagement-focused sections. But commercial pages also need the right visual elements, comparison functions, trust signals, and page-level interaction patterns. A commercial page can rank better temporarily, but it won’t always sustain long-term rankings if the necessary visual and functional elements are missing. The actual subject of the project is not safari. Safari is only used here as a replacement to explain the methodology.
